content.claim: Cross-national evidence from multiple studies shows early tracking (age 10, as practiced in Austria, Germany, Hungary, and Slovakia) increases between-school achievement variance and social inequality without raising average achievement.
content.context: Hungary's early-selection system at ages 10–12 increases achievement inequality and socio-economic stratification without raising average performance, and similar learning gains to Portugal's are achievable through non-structural reforms (curriculum clarity, assessment transparency, retention reduction, targeted support) within a comprehensive framework.
limitations: The v1 source string was not re-verified during migration.
content.population: Population described in the migrated claim
content.time_scope: As reported in the v1 source corpus
